返回本批次

用最糟糕的语言构建 Z 机器 – 白胡子王国

Building a Z-Machine in the worst possible language – Whitebeard's Realm

whitebeard.blog 作者 techbelly 分数 22 评论 2 2026/04/12 10:57
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. Z-machine 是 Infocom 制造的 20 世纪 80 年代早期虚拟机，允许他们一次性编译文本冒险游戏并在多种架构上运行。这是一个好技巧：如果您有 10 款游戏需要在 10 种不同的计算机架构上运行（啊，过去的美好时光！），您可以将工作量从 10 x 10 编译噩梦减少到 10 台 Z 机器 + 10 编译噩梦。当您添加更多架构和更多游戏时，数学就会变得复杂。有许多现代 Z 机器，一部分是软件考古学，一部分是写给这个文本冒险时代的情书。我一直想制作一台自己的 Z 机器，现在我做到了！我选择的语言是 Elm。但是……Z 机器是一台具有独立堆栈的直接内存访问机器，设计用于在非常普通的机器上运行。实现这一点的最糟糕的语言是一种所有数据结构都是不可变的并且没有函数有副作用的纯语言。像榆树。但我们就这样了。举个例子来说明这个想法是多么愚蠢：想象一下你将内存存储为字节数组。要以非纯语言向内存写入一点，您可能需要一个函数来获取内存、地址和值。然后它将值写入内存，可能返回成功或错误代码。我们无法在 Elm 中做到这一点。相反，您将拥有一个采用相同参数但随后返回字节已更改的新内存的函数。原始参数不受影响。对于看起来像大量数据复制和性能/内存灾难的 Z-machine 模拟器 - 只需设置一个字节。最初我认为如果没有大量巧妙的数据结构，它就不实用。但实际上，事实证明Elm已经在那里了。数组由持久数据结构（RRB trie 变体）支持，在切片和附加时可以实现更好的性能。最初的测试似乎证明了这一点，所以我继续告诉自己我没有我想象的那么疯狂。几周后 - 编写测试，研究 Z-machine 规范（仅文本编码就花了几天时间来理解），试图找到不可变的模式来支持可变操作 - 我有一个可以运行 .z3 infocom 游戏（版本 3，最常见的版本）的工作 Z-machine，并且通过了 Z-machine 的捷克合规性测试。漂亮，但它有效，而且性能足够好，可以成为在浏览器或其他地方构建良好的交互式小说播放器的可行方法。我想从中做的主要事情是做一些有趣的客户端实验（例如 if-pal）。为了让这一切变得更容易，我尽了最大努力为库提供了一个干净的界面来单步执行和处理事件。是时候学习一些 elm 代码了。抱歉。加载 .z3 文件并返回 ZMachine:ZMachine.load : Bytes -> Result String ZMachine 只需一行即可 elm 中没有无限循环，因此我们运行机器执行最大数量的指令，并期望它告诉我们是否尚未完成。我们返回更新后的机器和 StepResult：ZMachine.runSteps ： Int -> ZMachine -> StepResult StepResult 为您提供一个/多个步骤的结果、输出事件列表和一个新机器：type StepResult = Continue (List OutputEvent) ZMachine - 我们运行了这些步骤，但还有更多要运行 | NeedInput InputRequest (List OutputEvent) ZMachine——我们需要从用户那里获取一些输入——一旦我们得到它，就调用 ZMachine.provideInput | NeedSave Snapshot (List OutputEvent) ZMachine - 用户被要求保存此快照 - 一旦我们完成它，调用 ZMachine.provideSaveResult | NeedRestore (List OutputEvent) ZMachine - 用户想要加载快照 - 一旦我们完成它，调用 ZMachine.provideRestoreResult |停止（列出输出事件）ZMachine - 我们完成了 | Error ZMachineError (List OutputEvent) ZMachine -- oops OutputEvent 可以是以下之一 - 要处理的主要事件是 PrintText、NewLine 和 ShowStatusLine:type OutputEvent = PrintText String |新线 |显示状态行 状态行 |分割窗口 Int |设置窗口 窗口 |擦除窗口 Int | SetCursor Int Int | 设置光标SetBufferMode 布尔 | PlaySound Int 我认为这使得一个非常简单的表面具有足够的功能来构建一个好的客户端。github 页面上有更多详细信息，并且在存储库中有一个示例 node.js/elm 应用程序，它展示了将其与 Zork1 副本一起使用的示例。如果您曾经想构建自己的信息通信客户端 - 事实上，谁没有想过呢？ - 这应该会让你走得很远。

Original Summary

  1. The Z-machine is an early 1980s virtual machine made by Infocom to allow them to compile their text adventure games once and have them run on multiple architectures. It’s a good trick: if you have 10 games that need to run on 10 different computer architectures (ah, the good old days!) you can reduce the work from 10 x 10 compilation nightmares to 10 Z-machines + 10 compilation nightmares. And that maths compounds as you add more architectures and more games. There are many modern Z-machines that are part software archeology and part love letters to this age of text adventures.I’ve always wanted to make one of my own and now I have!My language of choice is Elm. But… the Z-machine is a direct memory access machine with a separate stack, designed to run on a very modest machine. The worst language to implement this in would be one in which all data structures are immutable and no functions have side-effects, a pure language. Like Elm. But there we are.As an example of how silly an idea this is: imagine that you’re storing the memory as an Array of bytes. To write a bit to the memory in a non-pure language you might have a function that takes the memory, an address and a value. It would then write the value into the memory, perhaps returning a success or error code. We can’t do that in Elm.Instead you’ll have a function that takes the same parameters but then returns a new memory with the byte changed. The original parameter remains unaffected. For a Z-machine emulator that looks like a lot of data copying and a performance/memory disaster - just to set one byte. Initially I thought it wouldn’t be practical without a lot of clever data structures. But actually, it turns out that Elm was already there. Arrays are backed by a persistent data structure - a RRB trie variant - that allows much better performance when slicing and appending. An initial test seemed to prove this and so I pushed on telling myself I wasn’t as crazy as I suspected.A couple of weeks later - writing tests, wrestling with the Z-machine spec (the text encoding alone took a few days to understand), trying to find immutable patterns to support mutable operations - and I have a working Z-machine that can run a .z3 infocom game (version 3, the most common version) and that passes the Czech compliance test for Z-machines.Some of it is not very pretty, but it works and it’s performant enough to be a viable way to build a good interactive fiction player - in the browser or elsewhere.The main thing I want from this is to do some interesting client experiments (like if-pal). To make that easier I’ve done my best to give the library a clean interface for stepping and handling events. Time for a bit of elm code. I’m sorry.It’s one line to load a .z3 file and get back a ZMachine:ZMachine.load : Bytes -> Result String ZMachine There are no infinite loops in elm so instead we run the machine for a maximum number of instructions and expect it to tell us if it hasn’t finished. We get back an updated machine and a StepResult:ZMachine.runSteps : Int -> ZMachine -> StepResult A StepResult gets you the result of a step/steps, a list of output events and a new machine:type StepResult = Continue (List OutputEvent) ZMachine -- we ran the steps, but there are more to run | NeedInput InputRequest (List OutputEvent) ZMachine -- we need to get some input from the user -- once we have it call ZMachine.provideInput | NeedSave Snapshot (List OutputEvent) ZMachine -- the user's asked to save this snapshot -- once we've done it call ZMachine.provideSaveResult | NeedRestore (List OutputEvent) ZMachine -- the user wants to load a snapshot -- once we've done it call ZMachine.provideRestoreResult | Halted (List OutputEvent) ZMachine -- we're done | Error ZMachineError (List OutputEvent) ZMachine -- oops An OutputEvent can be one of the following - the main ones to handle are PrintText, NewLine and ShowStatusLine:type OutputEvent = PrintText String | NewLine | ShowStatusLine StatusLine | SplitWindow Int | SetWindow Window | EraseWindow Int | SetCursor Int Int | SetBufferMode Bool | PlaySound Int I think that makes for a very simple surface with enough functionality to build a good client.There are more details on the github page and in the repository there’s an example node.js/elm app that shows an example of using it with a copy of Zork1. If you’ve ever wanted to build your own infocom client - and really, who hasn’t? - this should get you a long way there.

评论（默认中文）

user3939382 2026/04/12 15:08
这太棒了。想必如果这在 Elm 中有效，Clojure 也能工作。对于那些不了解这些游戏的人来说，有一个非常酷的利基社区。 Vespers 是我玩过的最好的游戏之一。查看 ifdb 的游戏。也有移动播放器可用。
This is awesome. Presumably if this worked in Elm, Clojure would work too. There’s a very cool niche community around these games for those who don’t know. Vespers was one of the best games I ever played. Check out ifdb for games. There are mobile players available too.
